item.title: Alex Garland and A24’s Elden Ring Movie Premieres in March 2028, Cast Includes Nick Offerman, Kit O’Connor, and Cailee Spaeny
item.content:encoded:
Ex Machina, Civil War, Warfare, and 28 Years Later writer Alex Garland is leading the charge on an upcoming Elden Ring movie being made by A24 with Bandai Namco collaborating - this much we've known since May 2025. Now, the latest update from Garland and company is that the film has a release date set for March 3, 2028, and the full cast has been selected, though it's unclear what roles everyone will be playing.

In a press release Bandai Namco confirmed that the film is in production (though that much was already obvious after footage of the set leaked online two weeks ago) and that one of the rumored cast members, Nick Offerman, would indeed play a role in the film. Beyond Offerman, we also got the rest of the cast revealed, which includes several actors Garland has previously worked with across Warfare, Civil War, and Ex Machina, though their parts have not yet been confirmed.

The rest of the cast includes Kit O'Connor, who will reportedly star in the film alongside Ben Wishaw. They'll be supported by Cailee Spaeny, Tom Burke, Havana Rose Liu, Sonoya Mizuno, Jonathan Pryce, Ruby Cruz, John Hodgkinson, Jefferson Hall, Emma Laird, Peter Serafinowicz, and of course, Nick Offerman.

It'll be shot entirely for IMAX theatres and it'll be produced by Peter Rice, Andrew Macdonald, Allon Reich, Vince Geradis and George R.R. Martin, who fans of the game will know wrote a short story to establish a foundation for the history and the lore of the game that Hidetaka Miyazaki and the rest of the FromSoftware team took and ran with to develop the rest.

The Elden Ring movie is now the second major live-action video game film adaptation set for 2028 after the Call of Duty movie got a June 30, 2028 release date just last week during CinemaCon. Though in the case of that film, we still don't know who will be starring in the film.

But like the Call of Duty movie, there are a lot of unanswered questions. Questions like, when within the timeline of Elden Ring's story will the film be set? Is it going to try and fill in a gap somewhere in the world's history, or is it going to try and re-tell the story that players experience as they journey through the main campaign?

The most important question though is perhaps how will Garland and the rest of the film's creative team be able to adapt the environmental storytelling that FromSoftware is known for into a beat-by-beat plot? So much of how players experience the story in all of FromSoftware's games, not just in Elden Ring, is defined by reading item descriptions, looking at the ruins around you, and piecing a puzzle together as best you can.

You're never given a complete picture on purpose, so it'll be interesting to see how fans of the game react when Garland tells his version of events, and if he's able to reach both fans of the game and those who've never even heard of it looking for an interesting fantasy adventure film.

What's interesting to note about this film though is that according to Deadline, Garland pitched the idea for the film directly to Bandai Namco and FromSoftware to get the ball rolling. It doesn't seem to be the case that Bandai and FromSoftware were actively looking for someone to take on a film, and instead Garland, who is a major fan of the game, brought the idea to them. So if Garland had something that piqued Miyazaki's interest, it could be quite the spectacular adaptation. At least, we can hope it will be.

About the author: David has been writing about videogames, technology, and culture since 2020, with a focus on reporting daily news across multiple publications, including GameDaily.Biz, GameSkinny, and PlayStation Universe before joining Wccftech in 2025. David started contributing as Canada/US reporter for Wccftech's gaming section in 2025. Besides being up-to-date on the industry's movements, he loves interviewing developers, reviewing games, and writing intricate essays about the symbolism and layered meanings to be found in rich narratives as he's done for publications like GamesIndustry.Biz, LostInCult, and others. Outside of games he loves movies, music, theatre, his hometown, and his family, though not necessarily in that order.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/elden-ring-movie-release-date-full-cast-announced-alex-garland-a24/

item.title: WonderCon’s Cosplay Raises The Game With Some Completely Stunning Fits
item.content:encoded:
WonderCon 2026 has come and gone, the colossal Anaheim convention now in its 39th year (give or take a Covid), and it continues to generate some of the best cosplay you’ll ever see. This year’s collection from our roving photographers at Minerablu features some of the most impressive fits we’ve seen in a long while, both extravagant and super-subtle.

Dispatch firmly establishes itself as a cosplayer’s dream game, and I think we’re going to be seeing a lot more from it throughout the year. Today we’ve got appearances from Blonde Blazer, Sonar and Coupé. There’s also your obligatory excellent Marvel Rivals costume and the undefeatable League of Legends, but also some really out-there picks as well. I cannot get over the Garrett the Garbage Man fit from the uncanny Jason Momoa look-a-like @aquaman__sd, which will make you do a double-take. But also that Eevee mascot outfit! It’s more professional than the professionals.

Even designs from incredibly common cosplay characters Kairi and Sora from Kingdom Hearts feel like a significant level-up from the norm, with both looking uncannily cartoon-like thanks to perfect wigs and makeup. And it’s just lovely to see eight different Mando players finding one another for a group photo.

As ever, you can see many more outfits from the convention in Mineralblu’s video below, or scroll down for the individual photographs.

Daredevil – Marvel Rivals

BioShock

Annihilus – Marvel

Ursula – The Little Mermaid

Spirit Blossom Syndra – League of Legends

Nico Robin – One Piece

Smokey and the Bandit

Garrett the Garbage Man – A Minecraft Movie

Megatron – Transformers

Gundam

Eevee – Pokémon

Sonar & Coupé – Dispatch

Drukhari – Warhammer 40K

Blonde Blazer – Dispatch

Mandolorians & Grogu

Magik – Marvel Rivals

Kairi & Sora – Kingdom Hearts

Watcher – Marvel

item.title: Crimson Desert Actor Admits Game ‘Lacks in Narrative’
item.content:encoded:
Crimson Desert has been on the market for just over a month. It dropped midway through March and impressed players with a massive, open-world environment with plenty to uncover, but many felt the game’s story was somewhat lacking. It drove down some review scores, with some writing that the game was inconsistent and missing some depth.

In a recent AMA on Reddit, the mocap actor and likeness behind Kliff, the game’s protagonist, became the latest to acknowledge the game’s holes.

Crimson Desert Has Plenty of Meat

Crimson Desert has gone on to sell millions of copies, and Pearl Abyss is busy pouring quality-of-life improvements into the game to address some of the more glaring issues. Many have argued that if the game had been released now, enough has already changed that it would garner stronger reviews.

However, one thing that can’t really be changed is the game’s core campaign. It’s lengthy and spread over many chapters, but some feel it’s inconsistent, has poor pacing, and suffers from a lack of depth.

That was alluded to by Trevor McEwan during a recent AMA on Reddit. McEwan is a performance capture actor and provided the motion and face for Kliff, Crimson Desert’s gruff lead, voiced by Alec Newman.

When asked how he feels about the plot of Crimson Desert, McEwan transparently said:

I think what it lacks in narrative it more than makes up for in other aspects. There’s also lore hidden across the world as well, if players don’t mind a bit of reading.

(Thanks to GamesRadar for the spot)

McEwan also revealed that he worked on the motion capture and stunts alongside a Korean team, which was responsible for securing the more ‘dangerous’ sequences. He contributed mostly to the cinematics and cutscenes that try to flesh out that fledgling story.

Admittedly, he said that Crimson Desert turned out ‘better than (he) had hoped,’ suggesting that while it’s not perfect, it did exceed expectations. Even the switch from an MMO model to a single-player game didn’t impact his role too much:

Other changes affected the overall story mostly. Kliff stayed Kliff throughout development for the most part.

Have you got around to playing Crimson Desert yet? Let us know what you think about the game on the Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more Insider Gaming coverage, check out the news that

Senior Editor and Esports Lead

Grant has been gaming for 30+ years and in the industry for 10+. You'll probably find him playing a post-apocalyptic game or an extraction shooter somewhere.

More from Grant Taylor-Hill

item.title: Xbox Indie Showcase Announced for This Week
item.content:encoded:
The ID@Xbox Showcase is back this week, with the livestream on April 23 at 10 AM PT.

Xbox has been gearing up for a bunch of showcases in the coming weeks. Just last month, we saw the announcement of Xbox Games Showcase 2026 for June, which would be followed by Gears of War E-Day Direct. However, players can have a taste of a smaller showcase this week as they wait for the big one.

ID@Xbox Showcase Features Apehlion, Solo Leveling Arise Overdrive and More

A new report by IGN reveals the date and time of the upcoming ID@Xbox Showcase, along with details on which games will be featured. ID@Xbox is an indie-centric event, so players will see several hit indie titles featured. From what has been announced, Mistfall Hunter, There Are No Ghosts at the Grand, Aphelion, and Solo Leveling Arise Overdrive will definitely be included, with more to be revealed on the day of the event.

The last ID@Xbox showcase was held in October 2025, and we saw several big indie games highlighted at the event. Some of the games featured at the previous event will arrive as Day One additions to Xbox Game Pass in April 2026 Wave 2, including Aphelion and Sledding Game. Other highlights included the likes of TCG Pocket Simulator, Mouse: P.I. For Hire, Invincible VS, and more. Let’s see what the 2026 iteration has in store for us this Thursday.

In other news, here’s everything that was announced during the third Galaxies Gaming Showcase. Additionally, Xbox users will soon be able to redeem Microsoft Rewards points directly on the console. Will you be tuning in for the ID@Xbox Showcase on April 23? Leave your thoughts down in the comments, and join the official Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more information from Insider Gaming, read about Escape from Tarkov receiving another meta change from the latest patch. Don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: The Coma 3: Bloodlines launches April 30 for PS5, Xbox Series, PS4, Xbox One, and PC
item.content:encoded:
• PC
  • PS4
  • PS5
  • Xbox One
  • Xbox Series

2D survival horror sequel dated.

2D survival horror sequel The Coma 3: Bloodlines will launch for PlayStation 5, Xbox Series, PlayStation 4, Xbox One, and PC via Steam, Epic Games Store, and GOG on April 30, publisher Headup and developer Dvora Studio announced.

Here is an overview of the game, via Headup:

About

The Coma 3: Bloodlines is a 2D survival horror adventure where players must outwit and outlast relentless pursuers, all while solving puzzles, gathering clues, and untangling a web of curses and secrets…

Key Features

  • Welcome to the Coma World – In this realm of shadows, where rumors and urban legends become reality, all kinds of dangers lurk. If a living intruder dies here, their body will be severed from their spirit and never wake up again.
  • Exotic Region for The Mystery Horror – Experience a unique occult and mystery atmosphere in the special local area of South Korea. Who should you trust, and who should you abandon?
  • Into New Locations – Leaving behind familiar schools and neighborhoods, you will embark on unpredictable adventures with new missions and locations.
  • Run, Hide, or Fight Back – Each of the three playable characters brings unique skills and perspectives, offering fresh strategies and dangers as the story unfolds. From stealth and survival to combat – you shape how you experience the nightmare.

Watch a new trailer below.

Release Date Trailer

item.title: One Piece Dawn of Liberation Is An Uphill Battle From The Start
item.content:encoded:
One Piece Dawn of Liberation is a new board game coming out soon from KessCo. Allowing players to create a crew of pirates and loosely cover the story of the Wano Arc, establishing your domain, powering up your party, taking the fight directly to Kaido. It's you and your friends against the board and the clock is always ticking as you're fighting to establish dominance.

One Piece Dawn of Liberation is a 1-4 player game where everyone will be working together towards a common goal. Balance is incredibly important in this game so there are variant rules for 3, 2, and 1 player games that look to ensure that the NPC forces aren't too overpowered.

Setting Up One Piece: Dawn of Liberation

To get the game set up you'll be laying out the game board, starting your Lore at 0 and Joy at 10, putting out the Kaido dice and two pirate dice, lay out goals in each region according to which act they come from, lay out each regions starting resources, and put the Act 1 boss pile in place revealing the elephant Jack to start on the board.

From the available starting characters each player will get an equally sized stack to pick their starting Captain and Crew from. In a 3 player game the only difference is that you need to make sure Luffy is in the game, and in a 2 player game and below each crew will have two captains and one crewmate.

Each character in One Piece Dawn of Liberation has a special ability that is active for the player who is using that crew, they have stats based on whether they're the captain or crew, a certain amount of inventory space, and goals that when completed will allow you to flip over the character card to reveal a more powerful version of them.

These character special abilities and goals were a lot of fun to see and they leaned really nicely into each character. Usopp is able to attack in adjacent regions with his ranged proficiency, Law can swap places with tokens from other regions, and Sanji gains food when fighting bosses. Goals include Nami needing to have Loot Petals used, Law killing 16 pirates, and Chopper needs to have two regions with 8 Samurai each.

It's very clear through the characters, their abilities and bonuses, as well as the Bosses and their themed effects that the team at KessCo was extremely careful about creating situations that were accurate to their story counterparts.

Winning And Losing

One Piece: Dawn of Liberation is a constant balancing act between making process towards your goals – gathering resources, Joy, and Lore, stashing resources to complete quests, and powering up your crew – while fighting off bosses, clearing pirates from the various regions of Wano, and fighting against the Kaido clock that's causing the game to slowly get harder. Act 1 should not give you any level of comfort as the game comes out swinging…

The main failure state that you need to be worried about while playing Dawn of Liberation is from your Joy counter. This decreases at the end of each round by one if there is any boss in play, and by one for each pirate in a region that isn't being defended by a Samurai. Your Joy counter starts at 10 and while increasing Joy will obtain you some special benefits, losing Joy will trigger some negative effects. Depending on how the dice roll and how abilities like a Kaido roll or Smile Fruit can come out you can very quickly lose 5+ Joy in a single turn with little to be done about it…

The Components Of A Round

Each round is comprised of three phases; The Enemy Phase, The Player Phase, and The End Phase.

During the Enemy Phase new pirates are spawned in regions according to their dice rolls, the Kaido dice is rolled to trigger effects like revealing a boss, adding more pirate dice to the pirate dice pool, or even adding pirates to each regions. Bosses that are active might also have abilities that trigger during the Enemy Phase.

To explain the End Phase next this is where the consequences of what's on the board takes place. Where you lose Joy according to the bosses or number of pirates that remain after the player turn.

The Enemy Phase is the foreshadowing of the consequences that will be experienced in the End Phase, and then it's the Player Phase where you need to do everything you can to mitigate how many consequences you'll receive while also trying to work towards your goals.

The Player Phase is where the Captains and their Crewmates act. The Captains can attack a Boss, attack a number of Pirates, gain Lore and pull a Petal or Smile Fruit, gain Lore and complete a mission, generate a number of Resources, or recruit Samurai. The Crewmate can destroy 1 Pirate, produce 1 Resource, recruit 1 Samurai, gain 2 Lore, or complete a Mission.

As with most aspect of this game you're spoiled for choice. There's always going to be a moment that you'll be wanting to kill Pirates, gain Resources, obtain Lore, and more. As you're scrambling to complete all of these objectives there's never a wrong option, but you will find yourself fighting between which might be most correct.

One nice aspect of this round is that all players can act in any order as they perform their Captain and Crewmate actions and also have the option to move. This results in stressful conversations during the phase as everyone can collaborate to focus on where they think their abilities will be best spent, and hold hands to pray that dice roll high enough not to ruin your intentions.

Knowing How Much You Don't Know

One of the hardest aspects of Dawn of Liberation on your early plays is that there are so many moving pieces that it's hard to understand where your priority should be, and you're also not aware of the kinds of ways that the game can affect you. Throw into that the different types of abilities and potential synergy between character abilities you could have a team that works incredibly well together or one that is fighting against itself.

An early example of this in my first game was my 3-player table working to build up food to power up Sanji, who requires there be three food in all regions. While focusing on this goal early Kaido summoned a boss that moved all food to a single region invalidating all of our progress in that task. Multiple turns worth of movement and resource generation was immediately invalidated. In a game where you're constantly in an uphill battle even that marked our journey for failure.

There were so many moments where the game's effects took place taking us from barely treading water to being underwater and unfortunately unless we had been able to predict specifically where pirates were going to be summoned or what negative ability was about to be triggered there's nothing that was in our power to mitigate or correct the situation we'd ended up in.

I do wish there was some level of recommendations in the instructions for synergy between characters or a more directed breakdown of goals. As it is, your first game (probably your second too) you're likely just going to lose… it will be a learning experience but you'll be able to start your next game in a much better place.

This lack of knowledge is even more detrimental for the events that conclude Act 1 and Act 2 as even if you're in some state of comfortability your strategies are immediately going to be turned over. Some mild spoilers for the conclusion of Act 1 but it ends with every player needing to send one character to Prison, and a new boss is summoned that will convert killed Samurai to Pirates.

Even if you have every region defended with Samurai the turn after this event triggers, the turn after that you might instead be looking at losing 5+ Joy with only half of your available action economy as there are three characters in the Prison.

Three Is The Worst Number To Play As

Interestingly, as I played with two friends it became increasingly clear that a three player game is the hardest way to tackle this challenge. In a four player game you've got four captains and four crewmates to start with and in a two player game you've got four captains and two crewmates.

The three captains and three crewmates of the three player mode is only handicapped by starting with three additional Joy. When an additional captain in two-player or an additional player in four-player could mitigate a Joy loss of three nearly every turn it felt like there wasn't enough altered in our favor to correctly rebalance the experience.

If you're going into this game I'd highly recommend playing it as a two or four player experience.

The Third Act

A unique aspect of One Piece: Dawn of Liberation is that while Act 1 and Act 2 take place on the larger Wano board after completing Act 3 you'll move to a separate board that represents Kaido's base of Onigashima.

On the Onigashima board the base structure of the game is the same, but you'll instead be fighting a few bosses and a Kaido superboss. While Kaido starts with 200 health, for each of the bosses that you take down along the way you'll also be dealing damage to Kaido.

Once again you're facing the unrelenting push of the effects of the game board as Kaido has a variety of abilities that trigger once he reaches certain hp thresholds.

This is the final push of the game and if you've made it this far you're likely going to stand a good chance but the difficulty will continue to stack just as it has from Act 1 to Act 2.

One Piece: Dawn of Liberation Has A Lot Going For It

There's a lot happening with this game. The balance between six different types of resources, fighting back against multiple foes, and the ability to have so many possible actions in a turn but only being able to complete two or three at a time.

There's an incredible level of care with the IP, creating a board state reflective of the arc, filling it with characters with relevant abilities and goals that is so faithful to the source. As a fan of One Piece even if every boss filled me with dread, there was also excitement to see how they'd affect the game at large.

Starting as a new player I think you're going to end up having to sit through a few games of loss and frustration as you learn what's important, and know what is ahead to be able to plan for it. Once you get past that I think the experience is a far more balanced one and then it's less about anticipating the unexpected, but having the knowledge to craft a strong crew and set objectives with expertise.

I'd also highly recommend not playing this game in a three player configuration unless you're going to add a house rule or two that will help you fight off the waves of pirates and obstacles headed your way.

A very tough battle is ahead of you for anyone who wants to embark on the journey of One Piece Dawn of Liberation.



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/previews/one-piece-dawn-of-liberation-is-uphill-battle-from-start

item.title: Vampire Survivors’ new spinoff switches genres but keeps the good vibes
item.content:encoded:
When Vampire Survivors first exploded onto the scene, it was pretty much all I could think about. The formula of jumping into runs, taking on thousands of enemies, and becoming absurdly overpowered kept me picking up the game again and again — Steam says I’ve played it for more than 60 hours. Over time, though, despite the game’s many updates and expansions, the formula got stale, and I haven’t played it in more than a year. But I’ve become obsessed with the Vampire Survivors universe once again thanks to the new spinoff Vampire Crawlers.

Vampire Crawlers — technically, Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors — successfully translates the Vampire Survivors experience into a whole new style of game. This time, instead of a shoot ’em up crossed with a roguelike, it’s dungeon crawler mixed with a roguelike deckbuilder. You’ll still play as different characters, take on waves of enemies, and craft laughably strong builds, but instead of fighting bad guys in real time, you hunt them down by walking through retro-style dungeon crawler maps and dueling in turn-based deckbuilder card battles. The maps still have a charming pixelated style — many enemies look like blown-up versions of the enemy sprites from Survivors, which is really silly.

The key to the battles is playing cards in increasing order of mana cost. You’ll start each turn with a certain amount of mana, and at the top of each card, you’ll see how much each card costs to play. On their own, even high-cost weapon cards rarely make a dent against your opponents. But if you combo your cards in increasing cost order, they can scale to become ridiculously powerful.

It’s a relatively simple cycle. I’m usually just counting from zero to three over and over again. But it’s incredibly satisfying to make a build that lets you constantly cycle through combos for superpowered hits. You’ll also have to manage cards that can give you more mana, improve your stats, or add armor or healing, and they too can be boosted by combos to devastating effect. And like with Survivors, making strong evolution cards by combining the right weapons and items is basically required for victory.

There isn’t much to the dungeon crawling, but that’s not necessarily a bad thing. While it’s novel to explore returning areas from Survivors that you only experienced in that game from a top-down view, in Crawlers you have a map that shows the layout of the entire floor, and you’ll quickly unlock items that reveal the enemies and points of interest. Having all that information is quite useful, though; it’s nice to be able to plan your route to enemies and treasure chests so that you’re set up well for the floor’s boss.

Also similar to Survivors, you’ll earn coins as you play, and after runs, you can spend those coins on upgrades for permanent progression. That includes new characters (which have different starting decks and abilities you can trigger during runs), stat improvements (like more health, attack power, or even the ability to bring additional characters into a crawl), and potentially game-breaking Arcana cards. Even if you don’t get a lot of coins in a run, you’ll probably still make progress on various achievements like using a certain weapon a specific amount of times, and those come with rewards, too.

More than 10 hours in, I’m feeling the same way about Crawlers that I initially did about Survivors; every moment that I can, I’m trying to sneak in a run. Given the similarities, I know this feeling might eventually go away. But for now, there’s no place I’d rather crawl.

Vampire Crawlers launches on Nintendo Switch, PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X / S on April 21st. It will launch on Android and iOS later in 2026.

Follow topics and authors from this story to see more like this in your personalized homepage feed and to receive email updates.

  • Jay Peters


https://www.theverge.com/games/913410/vampire-survivors-new-spinoff-switches-genres-but-keeps-the-good-vibes

item.title: The shake up to Arc Raiders Expeditions may turn the extraction shooter into a week-long bloodbath, but it could pair well with the "significant chang
item.content:encoded:
You'll miss the days of FOMO

Embark Studios are making a big change to Arc Raiders Expeditions, the recurring event where players opt to reset most of their account data in return for some sweet, sweet skill points and buffs. It may seem like a small change – with you earning rewards for damage dealt within the event, instead of wealth accumulated – but it could turn the game that's become known for its friendly, helpful community into a bit of a bloodbath.

Expeditions are Embark Studios way of resetting powerful players back to zero so they avoid creating a massive yawning gap between the haves and the have nots. Every few months the studio announce a new exhibition which only high level players can volunteer for and to take part they must donate a massive amount of resources and valuable gear. These donations are to build a caravan that takes your character away from the city of Speranza never to be seen again. After the Expedition, you create a new character but you start this fresh life with the items, skill points, and buffs rewarded for your sacrifice.

How much you are rewarded for sacrificing your character is determined by how well you perform during the Expedition window itself. In the past, participating players would earn skill points for every 600,000 credits-worth of gear they had in their stash when the Expedition ended. Though, this created a problem as no one was taking their valuable loot into battle. During the week of the Expedition, participating players would lean on free loadouts because it wasn't worth wasting high-value gear. It made for a much less lively game, too, as high level players were also just looking to escape the game and start a new run as soon as they had filled their knapsack with loot.

To address this in the upcoming Expedition, which starts on April 28th, you won't earn skill points for the value of your stash when the event ends on May 4th, but how much damage you dished out during the week.

"We know that grinding for monetary value isn't the most exciting experience," Embark Studios wrote in the post announcing this change. "By introducing damage-based objectives and trials instead of stash value, we're providing more freedom and variety in how you can complete the Expedition, encouraging the use of loot rather than hoarding it throughout the 5-day window. Above all, we want expeditions to be fun and not an endless grind. We believe these changes create a fair and engaging challenge that keeps everyone active across the full 5 days."

Naturally, though, it means all those players who were cautiously holding back their best weapons in the last event will, instead, be bringing every gun to the party and looking for all the ways they can deal damage during the event. April 28th - May 4th may be a time to keep your head down if you're just looking for a nice hike in a robot apocalypse.

Back in January design director Virgil Watkins did say he wanted more people to get involved with Expeditions, so perhaps this free-for-all is exactly what he had in mind.

It's interesting to see the Arc Raiders team make adjustments now that they can see how their earlier design decisions have influenced player behaviour. By picking stash value, they encouraged cautious play that for the period of the event made their game a little less exciting for everyone.

I was listening to an interview with Watkins last week where he talks about potential changes to the skill tree because it currently doesn't fit the way players are playing the game. "It was a tough problem space for us and I don't think by any means we nailed it," he told AIAS's Game Makers Notebook. "There are definitely skills in there that are under-serving their purpose."

The problem Embark faced when they were designing it was making a skill tree that fit both sides of the Arc Raiders, the PvE and the PvP. While Watkins says he doesn't like skills that only deliver "small statistical changes", if they locked whole abilities within the skill tree, then it could make one player significantly more capable than another. "Do we really want someone who invests in this wing of the skill tree [to be] obviously more capable in PvP? Because they chose those [skills] they're going to win over someone who invested in the red tree instead?"

Watkins says they're currently "evaluating some pretty significant changes" to the skill trees to make it a more meaningful set of decisions. At the moment skills have little impact on how you play, and Watkins wants it to help you determine a playstyle. "Do I want to be the sneaky looter, or the guy who wants to be super agile and maneuver around and things like that?" he said. "I think we moved toward that ambition pretty well, but there's more to do."

What this could mean in the future, though, if the skill tree does get a big overhaul, is that players who take part in the Expedition could experiment with drastically different playstyles after each account wipe. And, depending on how violent they are in the event itself, they can net themselves a tidy packet of skill points to invest into their new character build as soon as they start.

item.title: Fresh Leaks From The Legend Of Zelda Movie Are Spurring All Kinds Of Fan Theories
item.content:encoded:
Sony and Nintendo’s The Legend of Zelda film has wrapped shooting in New Zealand, and while we’re still just over a year out from its May 7, 2027 release, the crumbs of set photos and other leaks have given fans of Nintendo’s fantasy series a bit to speculate about. What little we’ve seen of the movie’s production doesn’t necessarily point to it being an adaptation of any one specific game, and some of the new images coming out of the set also suggest that it may draw on multiple sources. So maybe the film will follow the Super Mario movies’ lead and be a bit of an amalgamation of different Zelda stories? 

Gyula Pados, the director of photography on the film, posted a since-deleted Instagram post with a few images from the set. However, before it was taken down, fans saved all the images and are now dissecting them on social media. The most straightforward one worth talking about is the photo of the movie’s clapper, which gives us our first look at Benjamin Evan Ainsworth as Link in his full green tunic with the Master Sword in hand. The design looks pretty similar to Link’s Twilight Princess appearance with chainmail underneath the green top. Notably, he also seems to be wearing a cape of some kind on his back, which is reminiscent of one of his Tears of the Kingdom looks. So even in the character design, it looks like the Zelda movie is borrowing from multiple Zelda games.

A foto de uma claquete do filme The Legend of Zelda postada pelo cinematógrafo do filme, Gyulas Pados, parece exibir uma arte com o Ben como Link (não vista?) e também a Master Sword
—
via @ gyula_pados_dp (Instagram) pic.twitter.com/WoCTaGOcZX

— 'Necro' Felipe #UnivNintendo (@necrolipe) April 20, 2026

The other picture that fans are dissecting is a group shot of the film’s crew standing on the set. We can’t see much of the background, but there’s just enough visible in the shot for folks to think it might be the Temple of Time as seen in The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time. It looks like there are engravings on the floor of the set that might resemble the Goddess Pearls from The Wind Waker as well. So what the hell is going on in the Legend of Zelda movie is anyone’s guess so far.

Yes https://t.co/HmtWmZ1UBq pic.twitter.com/xVzFjQDFoK

— 𝐙𝐄𝐋𝐃𝐂𝐀𝐒𝐓 (@ZeldcastPodcast) April 20, 2026

If you know anything about Zelda lore, you know this is (almost) all connected anyway, so it’s entirely possible the film is using series-wide worldbuilding as set dressing for an otherwise standalone story. A story synopsis was added to the movie’s IMDb page, but it was a pretty general description of a Zelda storyline and hasn’t appeared anywhere else, so take it with a grain of salt.

A live-action adaptation of the video game franchise. ‘The Legend of Zelda’ follows Link, a young warrior destined to protect the magical kingdom of Hyrule from the forces of darkness. The land is under threat from Ganon, a ruthless warlord who seeks the Triforce-an ancient relic said to grant limitless power. To stop him, Link must embark on a perilous journey, battling monstrous creatures, exploring treacherous dungeons, and solving intricate puzzles to uncover sacred artifacts that can aid him in his quest.

While Pados’ post was clearly sent out too early and without corporate approval, we’ll likely see more official promo and story clarification in the coming months now that the Zelda movie has wrapped filming. At this point, the only official casting we know of is Ainsworth as Link and Bo Bragason as Zelda, though some set photos have fans speculating that Severance actor Dichen Lachman may be playing Impa, a member of the Sheikah tribe and also typically Zelda’s advisor, or in some cases, bodyguard.



https://kotaku.com/legend-of-zelda-movie-set-photos-leak-link-temple-time-2000689021

item.title: Is Invincible VS On PlayStation, Xbox, Or Nintendo Switch? All Console Platforms, Explained
item.content:encoded:
Fighting games have always played best with a controller, and that’s made consoles the perfect home for the genre. Now, with Invincible VS fast approaching, it’s got fans questioning which platforms it’ll be playable on.

18 characters will be available at launch, and the game’s set to bring the same level of violence and gore as the show and the comics. It looks like a perfect treat for fans of the franchise, but if you want to play, you’d better make sure it’s releasing on your console of choice.

For all the details surrounding which platforms Invincible VS is releasing on, Insider Gaming has got you covered.

Is Invincible VS On PlayStation?

Good news, PlayStation fans, as Invincible VS will be launching on the PS5 on release day.

When it comes to consoles, PlayStation has to have the most diverse library out there. From its suite of generation-defining first-party titles to its wide range of third-party games, if a game’s coming to console, it usually winds up on the PlayStation 5.

  • More Insider Gaming: Invincible VS Release Date Countdown & Exact Start Times

And the same can be said for Invincible VS. The 3v3 tag-fighter is heading straight to PS5s on launch day, meaning any PlayStation fan can start squaring up to Conquest the moment the game drops—but don’t let it be a mistake!

Is Invincible VS On Xbox?

Don’t worry, Xbox fans, you aren’t being left out, as Invincible VS will be launching on both the Xbox Series X|S.

It’s not always a guarantee that a third-party game on PlayStation will also arrive on Xbox, but thankfully, it’s the case with Invincible VS. Better yet, there will be seamless crossplay between each console, so there’s no excuse for your friends to turn down your battle requests.

Is Invincible VS On Nintendo Switch?

This is where the bad news arrives: sorry, Nintendo Switch users, Invincible VS will not be playable on any Nintendo Switch consoles at launch.

Whilst it may not arrive at launch, that’s not to say that it couldn’t appear in the future. Nintendo is constantly bringing new titles onto its system, with Cyberpunk 2077 and Resident Evil 9 being two shining examples.

  • More Insider Gaming: Is NTE: Neverness to Everness on PlayStation, Xbox & PC?

If they can make those work on a Switch 2, there’s always hope that Invincible VS could be ported, too. But as things stand, if you want to play Invincible VS on a console at launch, you’ll need access to a PS5 or Xbox Series X|S.

What platform would you want to play Invincible VS on? Share your thoughts in the official Insider Gaming Discord Server.

For more Insider Gaming platform guides, check out the following:

  • Forza Horizon 6
  • Saros
  • Windrose

Charlie is one of Insider Gaming's Guides Writers who's been in the gaming industry for a little over a year. Unfortunately for him, he enjoys every video game genre out…

More from Charlie Champion

item.title: Xbox Game Pass April 2026 Wave 2 Titles Revealed
item.content:encoded:
The second Wave of games coming to Xbox Game Pass in April 2026 has been revealed, including Aphelion, Kiln, and more.

We saw some really big names added to Xbox Game Pass in Wave 1, including Hades 2, Replaced, Football Manager 26, and more. Wave 2 doesn’t have the biggest names; however, there is a good collection of titles for players to try out. The games will be added from April 21 to May 5, 2026.

Xbox Game Pass April 2026 Wave 2 Features Final Fantasy 5, Aphelion, and More

As mentioned above, April 2026 Wave 2 for Xbox Game Pass doesn’t have the flashiest titles, but it does have a fair few for players to spend their time on. Players will get to explore several Day One titles, including Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivor, Kiln, Aphelion, and more. There’s a classic entry reserved for this Wave as well, with Final Fantasy 5 arriving on May 5. Check out all the games included in Wave 2 below:

  • Little Rocket Lab (Cloud, Console, and PC) – April 21
  • Sopa: Tale of the Stolen Potato (Cloud, Console, Handheld, and PC) – April 21
  • Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – April 21 (Day One)
  • Kiln (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – April 23 (Day One)
  • Aphelion (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – April 28 (Day One)
  • Trepang2 (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – April 29
  • Heroes of Might & Magic: Olden Era (Game Preview) (PC) – April 30 (Day One)
  • Sledding Game (Game Preview) (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – April 30 (Day One)
  • TerraTech Legion (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – April 30
  • Final Fantasy V (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – May 5

Xbox Game Pass Games Leaving in April

As and when we see new games being added to the service, we also see some games leave the collection. Players will see 10 games removed from Xbox Game Pass on April 30, including Dragon Ball Xenoverse 2, Goat Simulator Remastered, and more. Check out the full list below:

  • Citizen Sleeper (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • Creatures of Ava (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • Dragon Ball Xenoverse 2 (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • Endless Legend 2 (PC)
  • Goat Simulator (Cloud and Console)
  • Goat Simulator Remastered (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • Hunt Showdown 1896 (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • NHL 24 (EA Play) (Cloud and Console)
  • Revenge of the Savage Planet (Cloud, Console, and PC)

The list of games that were added and will leave Game Pass in April was revealed in a post on Xbox Wire.

In other news, Xbox’s rumoured first-party tier will support cloud gaming, but will feature monthly time limits. Additionally, CEO Asha Sharma admitted that Xbox Game Pass is too expensive for players. What are your thoughts on the Xbox Game Pass April 2026 Wave 2 additions? Leave your thoughts down in the comments, and join the official Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more information from Insider Gaming, read about Tarkov Arena’s biggest patch yet. Don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: Bungie’s Limited Edition Marathon Controller Just Got a Hefty Price Cut at Amazon and Walmart
item.content:encoded:
Bungie’s Limited Edition Marathon Controller Just Got a Hefty Price Cut at Amazon and Walmart

It's an undeniably cool controller, in all fairness.

Bungie’s limited-edition Marathon DualSense controller has dropped in price far quicker than expected, and it’s not a small cut either.

Just weeks after launch, the PS5 gamepad is down 18%, now sitting at $69.68 at both Amazon and Walmart, which is a new low for the design.

PS5 DualSense Wireless Controller - Marathon Limited Edition

  • Get it at Amazon - $69.68
  • Get it at Walmart - $69.68

The controller ties into Marathon, Bungie's newly released extraction shooter. To mark the launch, Sony rolled out a small range of themed PS5 accessories back in March, with this special edition DualSense front and centre.

Design-wise, it’s more restrained than most limited-edition pads.

The base is the standard white shell you’d expect from a DualSense controller, but it’s layered with black and neon green Marathon iconography across the grips and touchpad.

Flip it over, and you’ll find the game’s logo stamped across the back in matching neon lettering, subtle enough not to be garish, but distinctive enough to stand out on a shelf.

It's purely a cosmetic upgrade, but at this price, it’s edging closer to standard DualSense pricing territory, which makes the limited-edition controller a whole lot more compelling than it was at launch.

Bungie recently released its 1.0.6 Patch update for Marathon, which focused on encourgaing players "to be nice" alongside plenty of other big gameplay changes.

It even looks like Bungie's attempt to make Marathon that bit more casual-friendly are so far helping, with reactions fairly positive since the update was pushed out.

For more on Bungie's ongoing extraction shooter, be sure to check out our full Marathon review or use our guides to help you with more successful runs.

Robert Anderson is IGN's Senior Commerce Editor and resident deals expert on games, collectibles, trading card games, and more. You can follow him @robertliam21 on Bluesky.

item.title: Elden Ring live-action film premieres March 3, 2028; full cast announced
item.content:encoded:
Kit Connor, Ben Whishaw, Cailee Spaeny, and more.

The live-action film adaptation of FromSoftware-developed fantasy action RPG Elden Ring will be filmed for IMAX and launch on March 3, 2028, Bandai Namco and production company A24 announced.

Production will begin this spring, with the full cast being as follows:

  • Kit Connor (Warfare, Heartstopper)
  • Ben Whishaw (Skyfall, Paddington)
  • Cailee Spaeny (Alien: Romulus, Civil War)
  • Tom Burke (Furiosa: A Mad Max Saga, Black Bag)
  • Havana Rose Liu (Bottoms)
  • Sonoya Mizuno (Ex Machina)
  • Jonathan Pryce (The Two Popes)
  • Ruby Cruz (Willow, Bottoms)
  • Nick Offerman (The Last of Us)
  • John Hodgkinson
  • Jefferson Hall
  • Emma Laird
  • Peter Serafinowicz

Here is a brief overview of the game, via Bandai Namco:

Created under the guidance of FromSoftware’s Hidetaka Miyazaki, based on a mythological story written by George R. R. Martin, this action RPG debuted in 2022. It allows players to explore vast environments and dungeons to discover the unknown and enjoy the sense of accomplishment that comes from overcoming obstacles and challenges. The dark fantasy action RPG has surpassed 30 million copies sold worldwide.

item.title: Here's your next batch of Xbox Game Pass games for April
item.content:encoded:
Microsoft has revealed the next batch of games coming to its Xbox Game Pass subscription service this April, and into next month.

Game Pass subscribers can look forward to the following games making their way onto the service in the coming weeks:

  • Little Rocket Lab (Cloud, Console, and PC) – 21st April, now with Game Pass Premium; joining Game Pass Ultimate and PC Game Pass
  • Sopa: Tale of the Stolen Potato (Cloud, Console, Handheld, and PC) – 21st April, now with Game Pass Premium; joining Game Pass Ultimate and PC Game Pass
  • Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – 21st April via Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • Kiln (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – 23rd April via Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • Aphelion (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, Handheld, and PC) – 28th April via Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • Trepang2 (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – 29th April via Game Pass Ultimate, Game Pass Premium, and PC Game Pass
  • Heroes of Might & Magic: Olden Era (Game Preview) (PC) – 30th April via Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • Sledding Game (Game Preview) (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – 30th April via Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • TerraTech Legion (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – 30th April via Game Pass Ultimate, PC Game Pass
  • Final Fantasy 5 (Cloud, Xbox Series X|S, and PC) – 5th May via Game Pass Ultimate, Game Pass Premium, PC Game Pass

Kiln is the upcoming pottery-based party brawler from Double Fine, the studio behind the likes of Psychonauts and last year's wonderfully surreal lighthouse adventure Keeper. "Create your pot, grab your friends, and join a team of colourful spirits who face off against one another in online arenas, where the ceramic creations you sculpt on a pottery wheel become the bodies you bring into battle," reads Kiln's official blurb.

Vampire Crawlers, meanwhile, is Vampire Survivors' deck-building spin-off. Our Bertie has already taken the Vampire Crawlers demo out for a spin, and while he enjoyed certain aspects, he wasn't wholly convinced. "Vampire Crawlers works within the confines of what it does, but as a Vampire Survivors experience, it wavers," Bertie wrote in Eurogamer's Vampire Crawlers feature. "There are moments - usually when fighting waves of creatures in a boss encounter - that Vampire Crawlers comes close to the intoxication of its source material. But the staccato nature of the game always seems to interrupt it. As a Vampire Survivors experience, it's fractured, and as a card game, it's not really detailed enough; I don't see much room for strategy or theorising here."

Then, away from supernatural cards, Aphelion will be Don't Nod's first human story in space. The developer promises a "powerful" experience on the game's release later this month. "As astronaut Ariane Montclair crash landed on the newly discovered Persephone, players must brave harsh landscapes, master survival tools, and navigate reality-bending phenomena on a desperate mission to reunite with her wounded partner, Thomas Cross," reads the official blurb.

As with every month, with new games coming to Game Pass, it does mean a selection of others will be leaving. On 30th April, the following with be leaving the service:

  • Citizen Sleeper (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • Creatures of Ava (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • Dragon Ball Xenoverse 2 (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • Endless Legend 2 (PC)
  • Goat Simulator (Cloud and Console)
  • Goat Simulator Remastered (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • Hunt Showdown 1896 (Cloud, Console, and PC)
  • NHL 24 (EA Play) (Cloud and Console)
  • Revenge of the Savage Planet (Cloud, Console, and PC)

For everything else in Microsoft's subscription service, you can check out our handy Xbox Game Pass guide detailing the many titles available.

Kiln

PS5, Xbox Series X/S, PC

Related topics

item.title: Nioh 3 to get free update with high-difficulty side missions, rarer accessories, and new skills
item.content:encoded:
Nioh 3 is a pretty loaded action RPG with plenty of things to do and plenty for eager samurai to discover, but since it's been a tremendous success for Koei Tecmo, free content updates are expanding its many features ahead of the paid season pass DLCs (which, for anyone that knows the series, is par for the course by now).

On 20th April, producer Kohei Shibata announced with an official post on Team Ninja's site that on the 27th April (a week from now) a notable free update to the game will add various systems and new content to the title.

"We would first like to express our gratitude to the many players around the world who have taken the time to play Nioh 3 since its release. We are currently hard at work developing future updates and DLC for the game," the post stated before explaining what's coming alongside the next patch. At the time of writing, the season pass DLCs are scheduled for September 2026 and February 2027 respectively, so it's good to also receive confirmation progress on those has been smooth.

The new free content includes "multiple high-difficulty Battle Scrolls" (side missions) which can be accessed from shrines, the addition of a Stone of Penance item "which increases accessory +Values and rarities in exchange for higher difficulties", and new skills given as rewards for completing the aforementioned high-level Battle Scroll missions.

The patch will also include "balance adjustments to enhance certain Graces", but specifics haven't been shared yet, so stay tuned for the full patch notes in the coming days. As usual, the team encourages players to keep tabs on the game's social media channels.

Eurogamer's review of Nioh 3 called the threequel a new high for the 'masocore' series and "the crucible in which all of Team Ninja's experience has been siphoned into." It's sold over one million copies (and counting) since its 6th February launch.

Related topics


https://www.eurogamer.net/nioh-3-free-content-update-april-2026-announced
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors Review
item.content:encoded:
To say that Vampire Survivors has been influential is a massive understatement – it quite literally spawned an entire genre of bullet heaven/Survivors-likes. There’s an argument to be made for and against that being a good thing, but there’s no denying that the game itself is stellar. The sheer dopamine of it all makes it a perfect game to just hop in and out of, but at its core, you’re doing the same thing and just watching numbers go up. It makes the brain happy. So, can Vampire Crawlers: The Turbo Wildcard from Vampire Survivors pull off the same kind of trick?

Vampire Crawlers naturally finds itself in a strange place. It has to follow-up an intensely simple game which had such a massive impact, and to be honest, I don’t think it’s nailed it to anywhere near the same degree. I like Vampire Crawlers well enough, but I don’t love it, and I think it’s because the simplicity of Vampire Crawlers is going to be stacked up against the broader genres of deckbuilder roguelites that includes the recently released Slay the Spire 2.

Now, to be fair, this is specifically a dungeon-crawler instead of just a roguelike, so you’re delving into different levels and navigating around them in first-person, picking which fights you want to take on to try and get stronger, and which ones you want to avoid because it’s not worth it. And the deck that you’re playing from features cards taken from Vampire Survivors itself, all with different effects like buffing you, dealing damage, or letting you draw more cards.

The main thing to pay attention to in each battle is your combo. From the hand that you draw from your deck each turn, the aim is to play a zero cost card first, then a one mana card, then a two mana card, and so on. Keeping your combo up can double the effects of your cards, making them substantially more powerful and dispatching enemies more quickly and hopefully before they can even deal damage to you.

Each time you level up on a run, you get a choice of cards to add to your deck, balancing between damage types, buffs and characters, and that naturally affects the likelihood of drawing suitable cards. You can also augment them as you go with gems, which might do something like add a buff, double the damage, or evolve them, a la Vampire Survivors.

You’re not just delving into levels for the sake of it, but to advance and get new levels and unlocks. Just as with Vampire Survivors, you’re practically assaulted with unlocks and achievement as you play, netting dozens of them on your first few successful runs, once again triggering all of those happy hormones. However, that’s sort of it. Vampire Crawlers is a lot of fun, but it’s also a game I don’t feel like is doing much more to go beyond that simple loop. It leads to a game that feels pretty samey after the first few levels and attempts, even as you work through the massive amounts of unlocks and stuff to do. The music does slap, though.

Summary

I don't need games to be life-changing, but I do need a bit more mechanical depth than what Vampire Crawlers has, and while you can craft some incredibly entertaining combos as you play, and each new characters offers new cards, the core concept remains the same throughout. I think this is a solid dungeon crawler, if not fantastic.

Good

  • Unlocks, all the time, forever
  • Getting a good combo going feels great

Bad

  • Feels a bit shallow
  • Once you get the combo system you've basically mastered the game

Related stories from TSA



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/04/20/vampire-crawlers-the-turbo-wildcard-from-vampire-survivors-review/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Vampire Crawlers Review - An Addicting Dungeon Delver
item.content:encoded:
No Clip is an excellent channel run by Danny O'Dwyer, and in one particular episode, he interviews the enigmatic mind behind the smash hit Vampire Survivors. One key fact that stuck with me from said interview was that Luca Galante, creator of Vampire Survivors, once worked in the casino industry. Galante implemented tactics used by said industry to keep players coming back --  to keep them addicted.

It's no wonder, then, that a spin-off -- called Vampire Crawlers -- follows these same principles. They want to hook players while providing unlimited amounts of fun and value. Well, poncle has certainly succeeded in that with Vampire Crawlers.

A Familiar Concept With a New Spin

If Vampire Crawlers is a proof of concept that the Vampire Survivors formula can be applied across a multitude of genres, this makes a very strong case. While it might not have as much content as its progenitor, the potential is just as boundless. 

For those who like the look of this game but aren't familiar with Vampire Survivors, this is also a safe spot to jump in, as it's a completely stand-alone spin-off. As for its gameplay, it is an exciting, accessible dungeon crawler roguelite with deck-building elements.

It's actually a very simple premise, with the first-person, dungeon exploration popularized by the Wizardry series. Explore dungeons, kill baddies, gain experience, and upgrade your arsenal; in this case, the arsenal is your deck of cards.

It's funny, because while I'm not completely averse to deck-builders, I'm pretty picky about which ones I play. Vampire Crawlers is most definitely the kind I like, where you go into a run with just a few cards on hand, and the rest you'll have to improvise and gain along the way. There isn't much forethought needed about what your "build" is going to be, because the choice of upgrades and cards you're given once you level up is entirely up to RNG.

In this way, it stays true to the Vampire Survivors theme of making do with what you're given, with even the most random builds having unlimited potential. Players familiar with the series will find cards recognizable, with them taking the forms of weapons like the iconic Garlic, as well as the Magic Wand, Whip, Cross, King Bible, and so much more.

Basically, the weapons available in the vanilla Vampire Survivors experience are present here, behaving in ways fans would expect while also conforming to the gameplay of a dungeon crawler. That also means that you can evolve these weapons -- excuse me, "cards" -- into stronger forms of themselves by combining certain items. The Garlic, for example, evolves into the Soul Eater when combined with the Pummarola. Once you have those two in your inventory, a level up might grant you the chance to combine them into a new, powerful weapon. 

Keep the Combo Going!

With Vampire Crawlers, every card is assigned a mana value, and you have a limited amount of mana you can use each turn in an encounter. Encounters can last any number of turns, and after you exhaust all your mana or manually end a turn, enemies attack you, and the cycle goes on. Without any upgrades, you start with just three or four mana, which isn't much to work with.

Vampire Crawlers tasks you with increasing a combo meter in order to maximize the potential of your cards. So, at the start of a round, you'll use something with zero mana, then one mana, then two, so on and so forth. Using a card as part of a combo always gives you a boost of some sort.

Going out of order is not optimal, though sometimes you have no choice, or it's necessary to pick a life-saving card like Armor to prevent death. Certain cards, like Tomes, will grant you mana, so you might even have to use that if you want to spend every card in your hand. After every turn of a battle, each card is thrown back into your deck for potential use, so you're really encouraged to just use everything you can. 

There's also a color system attributed to each card, though this is one element that isn't well-explained and difficult for me as someone who is colorblind. For example, the aforementioned tomes are Purple cards, while attacks are Red. Later on in runs, you can have a lot of cards in your hands to the point that it can be a little difficult to manage. I imagine the color system helps alleviate this for some people, but not necessarily for me.

There are a number of ways to increase the effectiveness of your cards outside of combos. Gems, acquired during runs, modify cards. Some of these modifiers are as simple as multiplying their damage by two or even three. Some gems might help refund mana, completely negate mana cost, or extend combos to maximize damage. 

It's through these gems that there is an insane amount of potential that I haven't even discovered yet. There are dozens of these gems, and like your level-up rewards, they're completely random. Part of the fun is making use of these wild effects and discovering new synergies. The Calcium gem, which adds a bouncy bone effect to your weapon? Sure, why not. Heck, put the Calcium gem on your "Bone" weapon card and just see what happens. It'll probably work!

There's also a central hub where you can upgrade these gems to appear more or less frequently. Likewise, other facilities in the hub allow you to unlock new characters, add gem slots to cards for future runs, unlock relics to add new twists to gameplay, Arcanas (returning from Vampire Survivors, of course), and more. If you're looking for a constant stream of unlocks and rewards, that's definitely where Vampire Crawlers excels. 

The hub itself could just be a bit more organized. These facilities are organized in a circle, and you have to go through them in a linear fashion. I wish the menu was a bit more intuitive so I could jump right back into my runs. After 20 hours or so, it just gets a bit annoying to scroll through the options, trying to find the Blacksmith, or the Inn, or whatever it is you're looking for. 

The Dungeons You'll Crawl

Speaking of dungeons themselves, Vampire Crawlers does a really neat spin on locations we know and love from the original game. A lot of the sprite work for characters and items is reused in this spin-off, but it's also a 3D game. Vampire Survivors is almost always completely 2D. 

Exploring iconic locations like the Milk Factory and Library from a 3D perspective is a really cool experience for those who have put hundreds of hours into Vampire Survivors, just as I have. The environments still have that retro, pixelated look, but being in 3D adds so much character to these locations. 

Though admittedly, the allure of these locations gets a bit old after a while. Perhaps my biggest complaint, then, is the lack of variety in dungeons. While upgrades, enemy locations, treasures, and such appear in random locations, there isn't enough visual distinction in these dungeons. 

Repetitive visuals are one complaint I have with most of the original Vampire Survivors locations too, and that is very much the case here. I'd like for more distinct dungeon layouts and, on top of that, there are only a handful of random encounters in the dungeon, so variety again feels just a tad bit repetitive.

By random encounters, I mean randomly placed treasure chests, statues to break (which grant upgrades like increased mana), vending machines, food vendors, and such. There's a lot of potential to throw some real wildcards into the mix to make each dungeon run feel unique from an adventuring, exploration perspective. 

One dungeon environment in particular leaves a lot to be desired -- bridges. There are several bridges that are themselves dungeons in Vampire Crawlers, and they're completely linear and uninteresting. From a gameplay perspective, they serve as a damage check, necessitating you upgrade things in the hub, so their purpose I understand.

It's just, as a dungeon crawler, I feel as though a completely straight line defeats the purpose of the genre, which is to explore the nooks and crannies of a dungeon, even if it means going off the beaten path.

Vampire Crawlers Review | Final Thoughts

Even so, everything you know and love from Vampire Survivors can be applied to Vampire Crawlers. It's addicting, gratifying, and constantly giving back to the player. It's the perfect kind of game to shut your brain off and listen to a podcast or watch a video on the second monitor, while also offering a fair amount of depth for those looking to exploit and break the game. 

After over 20 hours, I've still got a lot to discover, so I'm excited for another 20 more, and here's hoping for some exciting content updates, which I also hear are in the works. If Vampire Crawlers gets the same treatment as the original, with tons of content updates, I'm very excited for the future. 

Vampire Crawlers was reviewed on PC with a copy provided by the publisher over the course of around 22 hours of gameplay. All screenshots were taken during the process of review.



https://techraptor.net/gaming/reviews/vampire-crawlers-review-addicting-dungeon-delver

item.title: The games industry will never "get back to normal", lament Tony Hawk devs Iron Galaxy, as they make another round of layoffs
item.content:encoded:
Company will "accept these current market conditions as permanent"

Tony Hawk's Pro Skater 3+4 developers Iron Galaxy are laying off an unknown number of people, as they struggle to "adapt to the climate" of a videogames industry that is refusing to "get back to normal". This follows a round of 66 job losses at Iron Galaxy last February, which were described as a "last resort".

Founded back in 2008, Iron Galaxy are known for supporting development and creating ports of games like Apex Legends and The Last of Us, but they've also made or published a few original games, including Videoball (worked on by journo legendaire Tim Rogers) and Divekick. According to their LinkedIn page, they have over 200 employees as of writing, spread across offices in Chicago, Orlando, and Nashville.

"Since we first got started, we have taken on many different forms as a company," reads a new LinkedIn update. "Throughout these different phases, we have changed our focus, our mission, and our size. We’ve worked with the best creators of interactive entertainment and made some games of our own. Along the way, we’ve seen the industry that we serve expand and contract in surprising ways.

"Ever since 2020, when everything about making video games started to change, people have been waiting for business 'to get back to normal'," the post continues. "These are the conversations we’ve had with colleagues and partners on our travels to the places where game creators gather. This year, we’re adopting a new posture to accept these current market conditions as permanent.

"Players consume games in new ways," it goes on. "Publishers have different criteria for investing in the development of games. This new normal has impacted all our partners. As Iron Galaxy adapts, we must make painful decisions about what we can be as a company. It’s impossible for us to sustain the team size that we’ve carried this past year, even after our downsizing from last year."

Iron Galaxy don't go into detail about what they mean by "back to normal", so let me blearily shovel in some context: they're probably referring to the gaming boom cultivated by Covid pandemic social distancing provisions, which saw many already-large videogame companies expand dramatically… only to cut jobs, contracts and projects in droves a few years later, after the lifting of quarantine measures.

Over the past few years, the biggest publishers have also sought to offset high blockbuster development costs and keep the gravy train going by investing in magic-bean technologies like NFTs and the metaverse, while funnelling vast sums towards live service games that could become 'evergreen' hits. Some of the company heads concerned, like Epic's Tim Sweeney, have admitted to being unrealistic in their financial projections, though few of them have faced any significant material repercussions.

The latest magic-bean technology is generative AI, which I will concede isn't as useless as NFTs, but has a bunch of fundamental issues, ranging from sheer cost in terms of hardware through overall quality of output to questions of cultural cannibalism and artistic autonomy. While much hyped, generative AI shows little sign of being the business force multiplier the suits are hoping for, though it's widely touted as an excuse to, if not actively lay people off, then keep team sizes small. It certainly doesn't seem to be offering much insulation against recent economic shocks like the US-Iran war, which has driven up fuel prices.

Again, all that's me extrapolating from the Iron Galaxy post, not a commentary on anything specific at Iron Galaxy. The company's co-CEO Adam Boyes has taken a cautious view on generative AI in the past, stating in 2024 that "if AI enters our workflow, it's only a tool to benefit those experienced professionals in the earliest phases of ideation".

I thought Divekick was cool, and so did Ben Barrett. Best of luck to all the Iron Galaxy developers facing redundancy.



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/the-games-industry-will-never-get-back-to-normal-lament-tony-hawk-devs-iron-galaxy-as-they-make-another-round-of-layoffs

item.title: Tarkov Arena’s Biggest Patch Yet Has Just Arrived
item.content:encoded:
Battlestate Games has deployed the 0.5.0 update across Escape from Tarkov: Arena. This session-based, hardcore shooter is the traditional multiplayer portion of the extraction shooter’s universe, offering fast-paced, small-form action over the expansive, open adventures found in Escape from Tarkov, the core platform.

The 0.5.0.0 update in EFT: Arena represents a massive overhaul to the game’s ecosystem. It adds an entirely new battle pass, new maps, refined modes, fresh weapons, hundreds of rewards, and much more.

EFT: Arena’s 0.5.0.0 Patch is Enormous

Escape from Tarkov: Arena doesn’t have the pull that the core platform does, but it’s still a nice supplementary title, especially for those already loving Escape from Tarkov. The team at Battlestate Games has linked the two games together in a few ways, which means what you do in Arena can benefit your ‘main character’ in Escape from Tarkov.

Recently, the team rolled out the Casus Belli event, designed to cross the wires between the two games, and it was a pretty big success.

In the most recent Escape from Tarkov: Arena patch notes for the 0.5.0 update, we get a look at just how gargantuan this change is.

It starts with the new battle pass, also named Casus Belli. This features more than 70 unique rewards, including weapon skins, new sights and attachments, fresh clothing, and full gear sets for players to unlock and use as they grind the levels.

Factory has been introduced to Escape from Tarkov: Arena as a new tactical map. It has a unique voiceover, courtesy of Tagilla, but to access the map, which includes new points of interest and exclusive rewards, you’ll need to buy it on the Escape from Tarkov website.

ShootOut has been reworked and brought back for this new season in EFT: Arena. This is a mini-tournament mode that offers fast-paced action among six teams of two players each. It has been fully redesigned and improved for the 0.5.0 patch.

Additionally, EFT: Arena gets several audio changes, including cutting down gunshot audio and the range of certain impact sounds.

To further flesh out the platform, Battlestate has introduced a massive 46-part task line in EFT: Arena. The first tier of this is designed to help beginners understand the game, while the Advanced and Hardcore levels provide missions for the more talented players in the Arena.

Here are all the new weapons added to EFT: Arena with this patch:

  • Colt M16A1
  • Colt M16A2 (in the Mk12 Mod 0 marksman rifle preset variant)
  • CGNL NL545 (DI)
  • CGNL NL545 (GP)
  • AK-308
  • AS VAL MOD.4
  • Marlin MXLR
  • KAC SR-25 (Taupe) (in M110 preset variants)
  • TheAKGuy AK-50
  • TKPD carbine

This list is in addition to dozens of armor items, attachments, and other pieces of equipment, all brought over from Escape from Tarkov to bolster the offering in EFT: Arena.

But that’s still not everything.

Meta score has been rebalanced, starter presets have been adjusted, weapon and attachments progression has been overhauled, unpopular locations in certain modes have been disabled from the map pool, deeper customization options have been added, and, of course, the game has now transitioned to Unity 6.

That means the team has:

  • Improved performance and overall client stability.
  • Fixed several issues from the previous Unity version.
  • Unlocked potential for further performance optimization and graphical improvements.

Before we wrap, here’s what we mean by the Meta score element:

With Patch 0.5.0, we are introducing experimental changes to the Meta score limits. Previously, the limits of each of the four bars had to be considered when assembling a valid preset, helping maintain balance and variety in EFT: Arena. At the same time, the system felt overloaded, especially for new players.

We are temporarily removing restrictions for three Meta score bars: Firepower, Defense, and Utility. This will give players complete freedom in building presets, but may result in unbalanced “meme” builds appearing in matches.

Are you still playing EFT: Arena? Let us know on the Insider Gaming Discord server if you’re excited about this massive change.

For more Insider Gaming coverage, check out the news about Escape from Tarkov’s latest meta shift

Senior Editor and Esports Lead

Grant has been gaming for 30+ years and in the industry for 10+. You'll probably find him playing a post-apocalyptic game or an extraction shooter somewhere.

More from Grant Taylor-Hill

item.title: Complete Saros Trophy List—How To Get All Trophies
item.content:encoded:
The Saros trophy list is in keeping with typical Housemarque trophy lists, offering a mix of challenging tasks, but difficult enough to make you feel accomplished if you can mop them up all and claim the Platinum Trophy.

Saros offers even more punishing, pixel-perfect roguelite perfection with Housemarque at the wheel. Living up to the sky-high expectations of Returnal isn’t easy, but I’m more than confident that Saros will be another world-beating, system-selling behemoth. If you plan on buying Saros, you should check out the trophy list below.

*THERE WILL BE STORY SPOILERS, SO BE WARNED*

How Many Achievements & Trophies In Saros?

The Saros trophy list is broken up into 45 Trophies, with the list divided into 1 Platinum Trophy, 2 Gold, 16 Silver, and 26 Bronze.

Insider Gaming has been sent the Saros trophy list ahead of time by an anonymous source, which is why I’m able to bring this to you now. For comparison, Saros’ trophy list is a seven-trophy upgrade on Returnal (only offering 38 Trophies), giving you even more incredible accomplishments to hunt for.

  • More Insider Gaming: Saros Review Embargo Date Countdown

Better yet, from the outside, there seems to be less focus on random trophies like the eternal headaches Returnal’s Past the Ruins trophy caused. Obviously, there are only trophies to earn, as Saros’ platforms are very limited.

Once again, this is your final warning before I reveal the full Saros trophy list to you, and it will contain all 45 Trophies, including story trophies and more.

All Saros Trophies & Achievements

Here’s every Saros trophy to obtain.

  • The Sun Is Forever: Mastered Carcaso and obtained every SAROS trophy.
  • Prophet: Defeat the Overlord of the Shattered Rise.
  • Bastion: Defeat the Overlord of the Ancient Depths.
  • Rhabdom: Defeat the Overlord of the Shattered Descent.
  • Legion: Defeat the Overlord of the Blighted Marsh.
  • Architect: Defeat the Overlord of the Desecrated Fortress.
  • Shepherd: Defeat the Overlord of the Acolyte’s Haven.
  • Priestess: Defeat the Overlord of the Cathedral.
  • King: Defeat the Overlord of the Yellow Shore.
  • Let Go: Complete all Epilogue requirements and defeat the King.
  • Never Let Go: Complete Act 1.
  • Into Yellow: Complete Act 2.
  • The Blazing Throne: Complete Act 3.
  • Strange Is The Night: Enter the Banyan Tree for the first time.
  • Shattered Glass: Complete every encounter inside the Banyan Tree.
  • Nightmare Strands: Enter and complete all 7 Nightmare Gates at least once.
  • Full Arsenal: Unlock all Main and Power Weapons, and defeat 25 hostiles with each.
  • Bullet Paradise: Dispatch 1995 regular hostiles.
  • Carcosan Cartographer: Use the Carcosan Modifiers to embark upon and survive 33 expeditions.
  • Soltari R&D: Unlock and utilize all Traits and Variants of a Weapon.
  • Break The Cycle: Absorb 2021 projectiles using the Shield.
  • Reinforced Carapace: Collect 20 Integrity Augments.
  • Eclipse’s Gift: Collect 70 Halcyon.
  • Carcosan Conqueror: Encounter and defeat 10 Alpha hostiles.
  • Catch And Release: Use the Shield to absorb 3 Power bars, then defeat a hostile with a Power
    Weapon.
  • Untouchable: Complete a Biome without taking direct, non-Corruption damage or losing
    Adrenaline.
  • Second Chance: Use Second Chance for the first time.
  • Approach Vector: Dispatch a shielded hostile with a mid-air Melee attack.
  • OverDriven: Dispatch 30 hostiles using OverDrive using a single cycle.
  • Countermand: Stagger 2 hostiles simultaneously with one Parry.
  • Enforcer: Damage 7 hostiles within 5 seconds with Handcannons.
  • Relentless: Land 50 consecutive shots on hostiles with Rifles.
  • Spread the Cost: Land 7 consecutive Shotgun blasts with 100% pellet accuracy.
  • Vicious Circle: Use Chakrams to damage 4 hostiles within 2 seconds.
  • All Shall Bow: Hit 3 hostiles at once with Crossbows.
  • Broadside: Defeat 5 hostiles with a single Prominence blast.
  • Atrophy: Damage 5 hostiles simultaneously with Dispiritor projectiles.
  • Angle Of Attack: Defeat a hostile using a ricochet Nova Lance slug.
  • Radiance: Damage 3 hostiles simultaneously using the Illumine.
  • Fatal Triptych: Die 3 times during a single cycle.
  • War Within: Reach maximum Corruption level and survive.
  • Never Enough: Collect 20 Halcyon in the Yellow Shore.
  • Fall From Grace: Use the environment to eliminate a hostile without shooting it.
  • Vincible: Complete a Biome without using the Shield.
  • Come Back Stronger: Die for the first time.

What do you make of the Saros trophy list? How does it compare to Returnal’s? Do you think you’ll be getting the Platinum Trophy? Share your thoughts with the team and me via the Insider Gaming Discord, where we do respond to messages!

Check out more Returnal guides:

  • Is Saros A Sequel To Returnal?
  • All Saros PS5 Pro Enhanced Features
  • Saros Release Date Countdown & Exact Start Times
  • Does Saros Have Early Access or a Demo?
  • Saros Game File Size and Pre-Load Date
